fed_monetary: The FOMC holds the federal funds rate at 3.50–3.75% for the second consecutive meeting, with Chair Powell explicitly warning that Iran-conflict-related oil-price pressures and tariff pass-through are sustaining core PCE at 2.97%—nearly a full percentage point above the 2% target. Powell attributes 50–75 bps of that overshoot directly to tariffs and signals a 'wait and see' posture on the Iran oil shock, while monitoring inflation expectations 'very carefully' before resuming any easing cycle. St. Louis Fed President Musalem concurs, characterizing the current 3.50–3.75% setting as 'appropriate' given upside inflation risk and flagging that the anticipated H2 2026 disinflation path has materially clouded.
labor_data: March NFP printed +178K, sharply outpacing the +100K consensus, while the unemployment rate held inline at 4.3%, and weekly initial claims of 207K continue to track below the 210K estimate—collectively signaling a still-resilient labor market. However, forward-looking survey data reveal growing corporate reluctance to hire amid Iran-war uncertainty, with S&P Global's flash PMI flagging further payroll growth deceleration in March. The risk skew is asymmetric: with hiring pace already subdued, any pickup in layoffs could translate into a rapid unemployment rate increase, complicating the Fed's dual-mandate calculus.
fiscal_supply: The federal government recorded a $1.2 trillion cumulative deficit through March 2026—the first six months of FY2026—running on a trajectory toward a nearly $2 trillion full-year shortfall against a statutory debt ceiling of $41.1 trillion. March alone added $249 billion in deficit financing (2% wider than March 2025), sustaining heavy Treasury net issuance that competes directly with risk assets for duration demand. The Treasury General Account stands at $751.4B with RRP near zero ($0.8B), leaving bank reserves at $3,129.6B as the primary liquidity buffer; the composite liquidity score at the 37th percentile signals conditions that are adequate but not accommodative.
corporate: Q1 2026 earnings season is tracking with notable upside surprise: of the 10% of S&P 500 companies that have reported, 88% beat EPS estimates—above the historical norm—and the index is on pace for double-digit year-over-year earnings growth for the sixth consecutive quarter, with total estimated earnings of $629.3B. Credit conditions remain benign on the surface—HY OAS at 285 bps and IG OAS at 100 bps are historically tight—but Loomis Sayles notes that risk premiums are being 'restored,' suggesting spread compression has peaked. WTI surging 4.25% to $93.04 on Strait of Hormuz risk premium and PPI printing 6.03% (vs. 2.6% estimate) threaten to compress downstream margins in energy-intensive sectors if sustained.
next_catalyst: The BEA's Advance Estimate of Q1 2026 GDP, scheduled for release April 30, is the single most important near-term catalyst: a print materially below trend would escalate stagflation concerns and force a repricing of the Fed's indefinite hold, while a resilient read would validate the current higher-for-longer consensus and likely steepen the front end of the yield curve.
sources: Fed Chair Powell warns tariffs and Iran conflict sustain inflation above 2% target, signals 'wait and see' on oil shock — Fortune / Harvard Gazette (April 2026)
